desc:
This is the western end of Hwain's Way, the long corridor that leads to the
King's chambers, armouries and smithies in the Seventh Deep, below the East Gate
of Khazad-dum. Above you Hwain's Way ends in an intersection, while to the east
the corridor runs - you might think forever - straight, smooth and gradually
descending into the darkness.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
A broad tunnel descends steadily from west to east. This seems to have been an
important road; the hard stone of its surface has survived the abuse of the
centuries. The walls are marked by simple columns carved in relief, which no one
has thought worth while to destroy.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
A large and seemingly endless corridor continues its long way through the lower
levels of Khazad-dum. The floor shows signs of great usage in the past, but
since the appearance of Durin's Bane, it would seem that traffic has virtually
ceased. The same can be said for maintainance, for the passage of time has
opened innumerable small cracks in the walls and floor.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
In this intersection the long corridor known as Hwain's Way bends from the west
to the southeast, where it continues its slow descent towards the King's
chambers. A secondary passage to the north abandons the main road and disappears
in the distance.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
This is one of the many intersections of Hwain's Way with some other lesser
tunnel. The main corridor continues its long descent from the northwest to the
southeast; from the entrance to the side passage, to the east, comes a cold and
damp air current that chills your bones and carries a strange smell.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
You have reached a bend in the long corridor that connects the western sections
of Khazad-dum to the King's Chambers, the deepest levels of the old Dwarven
city. Hwain's Way seems to go on endlessly both to the east and to the
northwest.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
An ornate doorway in the northern wall contrasts sharply with the usually
featureless walls of the interminable corridor that extends itself to the west
and the east. The smell coming from the north, and the crude symbols scratched
in coal on the wall, also contrast with the elegance of the Dwarven portal.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
The long corridor known as Hwain's Way stretches its way in a west-east
direction. Both to the west and to the east, the otherwise featureless rough
walls of the tunnel are interrupted by intersections. You can sometimes hear
strange growling sounds coming from the west. Who, or what, is making those
noises is not certain: their source, however, is not likely to be friendly
towards intruders.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
You are standing at a T junction between two important corridors. One, that runs
as usual from the west to the east, is Hwain's Way. The other corridor, which
begins behind a portal on the northern wall, climbs gently and soon bends to the
east. The latter corridor's name was forgotten in the years since the awakening
of the Valarauko, but, judging from its size, it must have had some importance.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
As you stop to remove a small stone from your boot, you begin to realise how
long the way from Hollin to Eastern Moria is. Hwain's Way, with its straight
walls full of small cracks, seems to continue forever. You might think that your
attempt to reach the Deeps, or to return to Hollin, is doomed to failure, and
that you will spend the rest of eternity walking along this endless corridor.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
You are standing in the middle of a large corridor below the Misty Mountains. As
you look west and east you cannot see the end of the tunnel; you are amazed at
the skill of the Dwarven miners of old, who managed to create such a large
complex of tunnels and to make it possible to live inside them without
suffocating. Yet, the same tunnels that once were used to extract the priceless
mithril from the roots of the Misties, are now home to a host of orcs - who
might take you by surprise any time!
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
Your footsteps echo in the vast emptiness of the large corridor known as Hwain's
Way. The tunnel continues straight as far as you can see to the west, towards
West Door, and to the east, where it descends towards what once were the King's
chambers and armoury. A smaller passage in the northern wall leads to an
ascending spiral staircase not too far away, and beyond, where it gradually
disappears in the darkness.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
The worn grey steps of a long and large spiral stair lead up from here. At the
base of the stair tunnels leave north and south. The southern passage is rather
short, and soon joins a much larger corridor, while the tunnel to the north
vanishes behind a bend after a few metres. The ground is cracked, as if a mighty
hammer had struck it violently: as you move, you are careful not to stumble and
sprain an ankle.
name: Bottom of a Long Stair

desc:
You are walking along a large tunnel, running from the east to the west, and
gently descending. The tunnel walls and floor are smooth, while the ceiling's
shape is a little irregular. A smaller, twisting passage leads south; its
ceiling is wet with small drops of water, the floor is slippery and the air
there is humid, as if the water of some subterranean river permeated the pores
of the rock.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
You have reached a junction along Hwain's Way. The large corridor, as usual,
continues its slow descent to the east and ascent to the west; a smaller passage
leaves north. The air coming from the northern passage is cooler than the air in
the main tunnel, and slightly damper. To the east Hwain's Way seems to have been
partly blocked off.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
Many huge, cracked blocks of grey stone fell to the ground when the ceiling of
this section of Hwain's Way collapsed, hundreds of years ago. Now, as the gravel
crackles below your weary feet, you watch carefully the irregularly shapen
boulders, since they all seem on the verge of toppling down and squishing you to
death in the process. Yet, if you want to cross to the other side, the only
visible way lies through the menacing rocks.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
You are standing in the middle of a seemingly endless large corridor, which
stretches itself in a more or less west to east direction. The air here is hot;
from time to time, a jet of even hotter air blows on your face from a merely
noticeable crack in the wall. Some tens of meters to the west, your way towards
Hollin and the light of the Sun has partially collapsed.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
You are walking along a corridor which seems to continue forever its straight
path in a west-east direction, slowly climbing to the west. A smaller tunnel
leads north and soon bifurcates into what looks like a minerary area. From the
west, if you stand still and are silent, you can sometimes hear some harsh
sounds. Are they voices, heavy steps, or something else? You will have to
investigate more directly to know.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
You are walking along a narrow minerary tunnel in the depths of Moria. To the
south this passage joins a much larger gallery; to the east it ends in a small
chamber. All traces of mineral ore seem to have vanished from here a long time
ago.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
You have reached the bottom of a narrow stairway, whose worn steps, roughly cut
in the grey stone, lead up and westwards. To the east and north, minerary
tunnels lead away in the darkness. The tunnel to the north is larger than the
other, and nearly horizontal, while the eastern tunnel slopes downwards.
name: Stairway

desc:
You are in the middle of a long, nearly straight tunnel, running north to south.
As you walk, you can hear the faint echo of your steps coming from both ends of
this gallery. Yet this is not the only sound you can perceive: sometimes, even
when you stop, something - it might be the noise of heavy boots marching on the
stone, but you are not sure - reaches your ears.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
You have the distinct feeling that you have walked through these small tunnels
for hours and hours now. Many passages look alike, and you have been forced to
retrace your steps - due to a blocked passage - dozens of times. At last, you
come to a chamber from which four major galleries depart. One leads west,
sloping upwards. The second is the largest, and leads almost horizontally to the
south. The third tunnel descends steeply to the east. Finally, the northern exit
is blocked by a pile of rubble.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
Following the main tunnel, you have reached a Y junction. The mines continue to
the north and east, while a third branch, to the southwest, ends abruptly with
an extremely large and seemingly bottomless chasm. A jet of hot air flows from
the depths of the earth, and makes you sweat.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
Dozens and dozens of small, twisted passages worm their way through the hard
rock of the depths of Moria in all directions. As far as you can see, the
Dwarves have followed the ore vein and all of its ramifications until no useful
materials remained around. After exploring the tunnels, you realise that many of
them have been closed off, or rejoin each other after a few tens of meters. The
main ways out lie to the west, to the south and to the east; the exits all look
alike to your eyes.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
At this point the tunnel you have been following becomes very narrow, so that in
certain places you have to squeeze yourself between the rocky wall and a pile of
debris. To the south the mine ends in a small room; a bifurcation can be seen
near you to the east.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
A narrow stairway crosses this small room. The steps descend to the east and
climb westwards into the darkness. A passage leads to a mine gallery to the
north, while, to the south, another tunnel has been sealed by a cave-in, perhaps
centuries ago, when the Dwarves were forced to abandon this place.
name: Stairway

desc:
You are in the middle of a network of small tunnels, leading in all directions.
Many of the tunnels, though, have been sealed by cave-ins or large holes on the
floor. The main gallery here leads north to south, but a few meters south of
here a large pile of debris blocks the way. The only secondary tunnel in decent
shape lies to the west.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
This place looks like the edge of a large network of minerary tunnels. To the
west a large passage slopes gently upwards; its floor was worn by the passage of
many loads of unrefined ore. Three tunnels led once to the south; now, one of
them has collapsed, while a very large chasm prevents you from entering the
second. As you look at the tunnel walls, you realise that there is very little
ore left here.
name: Exhausted Mines

desc:
You have reached the top of a smooth-walled tunnel which slopes down gently to
the south. A rougher tunnel, nearly horizontal, leaves east, entering a maze of
smaller caves, while a short, straight stair leads up to a tiny room. The gentle
sound of flowing water comes from above.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
The main passage here bends north and west, while a secondary tunnel plunges
down to the south. Cold air blows through this latter tunnel, whistling a
strange sound. The western tunnel slopes down gently, while to the north the
corridor rises somewhat more steeply.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
You are walking along a large tunnel deep below the peaks of Caradhras. The
corridor gently descends to the west and climbs to the east. Gusts of hot air
blow into your face from some small cracks in the floor and walls.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
The main corridor, climbing from the west to the east, meets here another
passage which goes north for a little while and then turns left. You can
sometimes hear a booming noise, similar to the sound of a large drum, and loud
cries in the distance. You recall hearing dreadful tales about the orc tribes
populating the halls of Khazad-dum: life under the shadow of Durin's Bane has
made these orcs particularly vicious and cruel.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
This is the western end of a large, smooth-walled passage that climbs gently to
the east. As you look around, you see that, to the south, the passage meets a
larger corridor - probably Hwain's Way, the main road from West Door to the
Seventh Deep of Eastern Moria. Water drips slowly from a crack in the ceiling.
The crack is larger than many other cracks you have seen, but it would be
impossible to force your way through.
name: Sloping Passage

desc:
A sharp turn in the passage is dominated by a huge statue of a Dwarf, placed in
a niche to the northeast. The once elegant marble of the statue has been defiled
by orkish scrawlings, and its arms lie in pieces at the bottom of the niche. The
soft stone walls of the corridor run west and south.
name: A Long Corridor

desc:
The cracked soft stone walls of this narrow corridor seem to grow nearer as you
proceed in your journey, giving you a feeling of claustrophobia. If you stop for
a moment and listen, you can hear a thumping noise in the distance, similar to
the marching of many heavy boots. The tunnel is still running in a west-east
direction.
name: A Long Corridor

desc:
The ancient walls of this narrow passage stretch themselves from the west to the
east. From time to time you bruise your arms against a protuberance in the soft,
cracked grey stone which surrounds you; this prevents you from falling prey to
the hypnotic effect of the seemingly endless walls.
name: A Long Corridor

desc:
You are standing at the western end of a long, but rather narrow, corridor.
There are many small cracks on the walls of soft stone. The corridor goes on to
the east; to the north it joins a damp passage.
name: A Long Corridor

desc:
Three tunnels meet here, in the depths of Moria. The southern one is larger than
the others and dry, while the passages to the north and west are damp. A very
strong smell of decay pervades this place, and seems to get even stronger to the
north.
name: T Junction

desc:
As you proceed inside this tunnel, the air becomes even more damp and
unbreathable than before. The stone below your feet is slippery because of a
very thin layer of water. As you look around, you realise that the source of the
smell of decay you noticed before lies here: the stench comes from a pile of
semidecomposed orc corpses. Above the grisly sight there is an inscription. The
tunnel continues south and east.
name: A Damp Side Passage

desc:
The tunnel here bends rather sharply west and south. A current of damp air comes
from the west, carrying the stench of decay, and plunges into a narrow crack in
the floor. Water trickles down from many small stalactites with a loud,
reverberating sound.
name: A Damp Side Passage

desc:
You are standing in a small secondary tunnel, which climbs slowly to the north.
Cold, humid air blows through nearly invisible fissures in the old cave walls,
chilling you to the bones. South of here the tunnel becomes slightly larger, and
bends westwards.
name: A Damp Side Passage

desc:
This is the southern end of a small corridor which climbs slowly to the north.
The air coming from the tunnel is stale, humid and carries a strange smell. To
the west, the corridor joins a much large passage, which, if what you heard
about Khazad-dum is correct and if your sense of direction does not betray you,
should be Hwain's Way, a large road which crosses all of Moria from the west to
the east.
name: A Damp Side Passage

desc:
The narrow space of this cleft in the rock makes movement difficult and slow.
The walls of this crevice are very wet due to the rivulets of water which snake
down the surfaces of the crack from above. This water collects in the narrow
bottom of the crevice and flowing as a small stream descends eastwards. A strong
air current blows down the length of the crack.
name: Rocky Crevice

desc:
A body of water fills this cove. The surface of the water lies undisturbed with
only a few small waves lapping against the craggy walls. To the north the dark
lake disappears into a large cavern. A breach in the west wall of the cove leads
to a crevice which recedes into the stone. A trickle of water enters the lake
from the floor of the crevice and a soft breeze also enters through this cleft.
name: Silent Lake

desc:
Still and dark, the waters of this underground lake look cold and uninviting.
Nary a sound can be heard, and the quiet splashes you make echo off the high
rocks of this chamber. To the east and north the glassy waters continue while
the rough rocks of a wall lie west. To the south the cavern becomes smaller, the
high ceiling descending towards the waters of a cove.
name: Silent Lake

desc:
In the roots of the mountain, the waters of the lake lie still, cold and dark.
Within its surface you can see your reflection but the depths are obscured by
suspensions underwater. Occasionally a solitary drip can be heard as water falls
from the few, small stalactites and stone promontories above. To the west and
north the huge cavern ends, and the rocky walls of the cave are gently lapped.
East and south the lake stretches out beyond your perceptions.
name: Silent Lake

desc:
A rocky ledge protruding from the northern wall lies just below the surface of
the lake, providing respite for those weary of swimming. The otherwise calm
surface of the lake is disturbed by the uprising of water from below. Down, deep
below, murky plumes of silt carried by the currents upwell and disperse
gradually, flowing slowly to the southern parts of the lake. The western reaches
of the lake lie dark and silent, few drops ever falling to break the quiet.
name: Silent Lake

desc:
This is a corner of the lake-immersed cavern which stretches north and east.
Slow currents below the surface deposit their suspensions deep on the lake
floor. The flow south suggests that an underwater outflow drains the waters from
the lake, though no sign of an exit is visible. The slippery walls of the cavern
to the south and east are impossible to scale, rising as they do towards a
distant rocky ceiling. Water occasionally drops from above but otherwise no
sound is heard.
name: Silent Lake

desc:
You are standing at a bend in a small passage below the Misty Mountains. The air
is extremely moist, so that some small, white fungi have managed to grow on the
plain walls of the tunnel. The stench of decay fills this place. To the south
the air seems to become more breathable, as the tunnel enlarges and meets a
larger corridor. To the east, after a short but steep ascent, you can see
another intersection.
name: A Damp Side Passage

desc:
You are walking along a large corridor running from the northwest to the
southeast. When Durin's Bane was still sleeping in the depths of the earth, this
was one of the most frequented roads in Khazad-dum. Since the Balrog came and
slew the King of Moria, most of the travellers along this once glorious corridor
are orcs, trolls and other foul servants of the Darkness: nearly all of the few
intruders from the outer world have been killed by the orcs, or, perhaps,
captured and sent to a fate worse than death.
name: Hwain's Way

desc:
A narrow stair begins here its slow descent to the lower levels of Khazad-dum.
The grey, worn steps almost remind you of the golden years of Moria, when
thousands of Dwarven miners laboured in tunnels like this one to extract the
priceless mithril from the roots of the Misty Mountains. Alas, the sound of the
pick is heard no more in these halls; the only constant sound, now, is the
flowing of water coming from the south.
name: Top of a Stairway

desc:
In the remote times when this section of the mines still yielded ore for the
Dwarven smithies, the miners probably used to come here to rest, from time to
time. A clean, tiny water rivulet flows from a crack in the wall into a sort of
carved washbasin, then vanishes again through a small hole. Along the walls you
can see many cracked stone benches, upon which are the broken remains of some
musical instruments. To the north a small tunnel leaves this room, while a ramp
of stairs goes down to the mine tunnels.
name: Miners' Resting Place

desc:
You are lost in some very deep caverns, hundreds of meters below the deepest
tunnels of the Dwarves. Here old, nameless beings gnaw at the rock, digging
long, twisted, cold caves in the foundations of the earth. Icy water trickles
down from the irregularly shapen ceiling, chilling you to the bones, then
collecting into small pools and flowing away through minute cracks in the floor.
Strange noises surround you, coming from all directions. Are they merely the
magnified echo of your own footsteps and your breath, mixed with the tiny
droplets falling, or is there some foul creature stalking you?
name: The Deeps of Moria

desc:
You are lost in some very deep caverns, hundreds of meters below the deepest
tunnels of the Dwarves. Here old, nameless beings gnaw at the rock, digging
long, twisted, cold caves in the foundations of the earth. Icy water trickles
down from the irregularly shapen ceiling, chilling you to the bones, then
collecting into small pools and flowing away through minute cracks in the floor.
Strange noises surround you, coming from all directions. Are they merely the
magnified echo of your own footsteps and your breath, mixed with the tiny
droplets falling, or is there some foul creature stalking you?
name: The Deeps of Moria

desc:
You have climbed a natural shaft from the caves below, and you think that, by
now, you should have reached once again the tunnels and the halls made by the
people of Durin. Above you the shaft becomes very narrow; the only way out of
this place seems to be a crack to the... south? The time you spent below made
you lose your sense of direction.
name: A Natural Shaft

desc:
The current suddenly slows and you feel yourself ejected into a large watery
expanse. The colour of the water clouds your vision, but you see above you the
surface. The currents still grab you but unless you want to swim against them,
you must surface here.
name: Source of the Lake

desc:
It seems ages since you were pulled into this underwater abyss. Your lungs now
scream for air and your limbs tire. Even your sight is gone, as the rust
colouring of the current means even your hand is unseen. The journey is also
rough and you bash into the rocky walls as the torrent takes a sharp bend. Your
only options are to let it carry you onwards, or to struggle in the opposite
direction.
name: Murky Torrent

desc:
With the water dissolving the rocks, and picking up sediment, you have no idea
where you are going. You can either let the water take you, or else you will
have to fight against the flow. The only thing of comfort in this airless world
is that there seem to be no bends in the watery-tunnel to bash into.
name: Murky Torrent

desc:
The waters in this part of the watercourse have eroded their way through rocks
which have deposited sediment in the flow. Looking back towards where the
torrent divided is only just possible. Ahead along the route of the water, only
a rusty colour can be seen.
name: Murky Torrent

desc:
This juncture of passages underwater is quite complex. From the north, a strong
current flows into this chamber, pitched at a steep gradient from south to
north. To the south and east is visible a dark tunnel leading down, the waters
obviously decending into it. Water from the north tunnel also makes its way
west, but due to the size of the exit, the water leaves in a more leisurely
fashion. Rocks and boulders of all size litter the bottom of the chamber along
the south wall.
name: Dividing of the Flow

desc:
This broken chamber looks to have once been part of a larger whole. To the
north, a strong current of water pushes its way towards you, through a
ramshackle wall of rocks and boulders. The few bubbles carried with the water
migrates upwards to provide a breathing space in the upper part of the chamber.
Strong eddies exist within the corners of the subterrean room and it is hard
work not to get swept downstream to the south.
name: Underwater Chamber

desc:
The tunnel winds north and south and water fills it to the top. No sign can be
seen of any small cave or side passage where contact with the world of air above
could be made. As the visibility is low, it is hard to avoid the small bits of
rock that periodically fly past. It is easy to cruise with the flow south,
needing little energy. To swim north against the current is entirely different.
name: Underwater Tunnel

desc:
The submerged tunnel rounds out here, forcing you to choose between one of the
walls for some guidance in the depths. The torrent flows down from the north
here, turning sharply to plunge off east. Much of the rock is pitted and worn as
small stones carried from upstream smash into the wall at this bend.
name: Underwater Bend

desc:
By some caprice of geology, this dome-shaped chamber remains dry and full of
air, while below the underwater torrent churns. The cavern has smooth walls, as
if at one time it had been scoured by the stream that now flows beneath it.
name: Dry Chamber

desc:
Onwards and on, the heady flow of water carries anything east. The way west is
now inclined upwards, though it passes under a rocky ledge as well. The water is
very cold and you cannot see far in either direction. As you do your utmost to
hold on to your possessions, you see no hope of any trapped air to refresh your
lungs.
name: Underwater Tunnel

desc:
In this jagged rock tunnel, the current of water heading east makes life easy
for swimming that way. Otherwise, a real effort to make headway west is
required. The water is not very clear, but it is good enough for you to keep
your bearings.
name: Underwater Tunnel

desc:
Below the turbulent eddying of the watery whirlpool, you can barely make out the
confines of the underwater current through the mass of bubbles, drawn down into
the depths. The fast current flows east along a rocky shelf, wider than it is
high.
name: Under the Whirlpool

desc:
Here within a a tall, narrow water-passage you are buffetted by the force of two
currents which meet here and flow off further east. To the west is a
continuation of the narrow tunnel. From the north wall descends a rocky tube,
carrying waters containing a few bubbles to join this aquatic corridor.
name: Underwater Torrent

desc:
The current speeds up as the height of the watery shelf under the whirlpool gets
lower in height. Just as it looks as if you will get forced into too narrow a
passage, the heavy flow arcs southwards and down a dark tunnel. The trapped
bubbles thin out and you can see a few dark blooms of life in the narrow crevice
at the back of the shelf to the east.
name: Underwater Tunnel

desc:
In the altering of the course of the water, the air carried with it has been
forced up into this crevice under the shelf. Although small, it provides a
useful supply of air for you and a welcome respite. The area still contains
plenty of water though.
name: An Air-filled Crevice

desc:
The flow of the subterranean torrent eastwards halts here as the rocky passage
ends and the water is diverted down a wide fissure in the floor. Though the
fissure opens up below, you cannot tell how much deeper the currents lead or
whether you will find a precious air-pocket from which to take a breath.
name: Underwater Torrent

desc:
This is the bottom of a wide fissure in the ceiling of a large submerged cavern.
As it is quite large, the speed of the currents is considerably slower than in
the tunnel above. The fissure narrows westwards, becoming too narrow to swim
into. Eastwards, the greater part of the cavern opens out but it is impossible
to see more than qualitative features.
name: A Submerged Fissure
